      • Experiment tracking
    • Cost Estimates

Training Workflow

End-to-end guide for training models on Rocket League replay data, from a local linear baseline on a small sample to full-scale sequence model training on AWS.

Prerequisites

# Install base dependencies
uv sync

# Install with PyTorch (required for training module)
uv sync --extra training

# Sync database from S3 (get latest parse state)
python -c "
from impulse.collection.database import ImpulseDB
from impulse.collection.s3_manager import S3Manager
db = ImpulseDB(s3_manager=S3Manager())
db.pull()
"

# Backfill segment boundaries (one-time, after first setup or new parses)
python scripts/backfill_segment_boundaries.py --db-path ./impulse.db --s3

Stage 1: Local Linear Baseline (Small Sample)

Train a linear model on ~200 replays locally. No GPU needed. Takes a few minutes to run.

import numpy as np
from sklearn.linear_model import Ridge

from impulse import (
    ReplayDataset, PreprocessingPipeline,
    FeatureSelector, PhysicalNormalizer, split_replay_ids,
)
from impulse.preprocessing import deserialize_boundaries

# 1. Load dataset and sample replay IDs
dataset = ReplayDataset(db_path='./impulse.db', cache_dir='./replay-cache/')
sample_ids = dataset.sample_replay_ids(200, seed=51)
train_ids, val_ids, test_ids = split_replay_ids(sample_ids)

# 2. Build preprocessing pipeline
pipeline = PreprocessingPipeline([
    FeatureSelector.from_preset('physics'),
    PhysicalNormalizer(),
])

# 3. Build X/Y arrays from training segments
X_train, Y_train = [], []
for replay in dataset.iter_ids(train_ids):
    boundaries = deserialize_boundaries(dataset.replay_info[replay.replay_id]['segment_boundaries'])
    processed = pipeline(replay.frames).to_numpy(np.float32)
    for start, end in boundaries:
        segment = processed[start:end]
        X_train.append(segment[:-1])  # all frames except last
        Y_train.append(segment[1:])   # all frames except first

X_train = np.concatenate(X_train)
Y_train = np.concatenate(Y_train)

# 4. Same for validation
X_val, Y_val = [], []
for replay in dataset.iter_ids(val_ids):
    boundaries = deserialize_boundaries(dataset.replay_info[replay.replay_id]['segment_boundaries'])
    processed = pipeline(replay.frames).to_numpy(np.float32)
    for start, end in boundaries:
        segment = processed[start:end]
        X_val.append(segment[:-1])
        Y_val.append(segment[1:])

X_val = np.concatenate(X_val)
Y_val = np.concatenate(Y_val)

# 5. Trivial baseline: predict no change
trivial_mse = ((Y_val - X_val) ** 2).mean(axis=0)

# 6. Train linear model
model = Ridge(alpha=1.0)
model.fit(X_train, Y_train)

Y_pred = model.predict(X_val)
linear_mse = ((Y_val - Y_pred) ** 2).mean(axis=0)

# 7. Compare
feature_names = pipeline.feature_columns
for i, name in enumerate(feature_names):
    improvement = (1 - linear_mse[i] / trivial_mse[i]) * 100
    print(f"{name:40s}  trivial={trivial_mse[i]:.6f}  linear={linear_mse[i]:.6f}  improvement={improvement:.1f}%")

What to look for

  • The linear model should beat the trivial baseline on most features.
  • Position features should be easy to predict (smooth trajectories).
  • Angular velocity and action flags are harder (discontinuous).
  • If the linear model is worse than trivial on some features, that’s expected — those features may need nonlinear models or longer context windows.

Stage 2: Full Dataset Linear Model (7,300 Replays)

Same approach as Stage 1 but iterating over all replays. Still fits in memory for a linear model since sklearn Ridge doesn’t need all data simultaneously — but building the full X/Y arrays will be large (~35M frames × 97 features × 4 bytes ≈ 13 GB).

Options for fitting at scale:

Option A: Incremental fitting with SGDRegressor

from sklearn.linear_model import SGDRegressor

models = [SGDRegressor(loss='squared_error', alpha=1e-4) for _ in range(n_features)]

for replay in dataset.iter_ids(train_ids):
    boundaries = deserialize_boundaries(dataset.replay_info[replay.replay_id]['segment_boundaries'])
    processed = pipeline(replay.frames).to_numpy(np.float32)
    for start, end in boundaries:
        seg = processed[start:end]
        X, Y = seg[:-1], seg[1:]
        for j, m in enumerate(models):
            m.partial_fit(X, Y[:, j])

Option B: PyTorch linear model (recommended for consistency with later stages)

import torch
import torch.nn as nn
from torch.utils.data import DataLoader
from impulse.training import ReplayTrainingDataset

train_dataset = ReplayTrainingDataset(
    dataset=dataset, pipeline=pipeline,
    replay_ids=train_ids,
    window_size=2, stride=1,  # pairs of consecutive frames
)
loader = DataLoader(train_dataset, batch_size=2048, shuffle=True, num_workers=4)

model = nn.Linear(n_features, n_features)
optimizer = torch.optim.Adam(model.parameters(), lr=1e-3)
loss_fn = nn.MSELoss()

for epoch in range(10):
    total_loss = 0
    for batch in loader:
        x, y = batch[:, 0, :], batch[:, 1, :]  # window_size=2 → input, target
        pred = model(x)
        loss = loss_fn(pred, y)
        optimizer.zero_grad()
        loss.backward()
        optimizer.step()
        total_loss += loss.item()
    print(f"Epoch {epoch}: loss={total_loss / len(loader):.6f}")

Stage 3: AWS Setup for GPU Training

EC2 Instance

Recommended: g4dn.xlarge (1x T4 GPU, 4 vCPU, 16 GB RAM, ~$0.53/hr on-demand or ~$0.16/hr spot).

For larger models: g5.xlarge (1x A10G GPU, 24 GB VRAM) or p3.2xlarge (1x V100, 16 GB VRAM).

Storage: EBS Volume for Parsed Data

Create a persistent EBS gp3 volume to store cached Parquet files. This survives instance stop/start and avoids re-downloading from S3 each time.

# On the EC2 instance, after attaching the EBS volume:
sudo mkfs.ext4 /dev/nvme1n1           # format (first time only!)
sudo mkdir -p /data
sudo mount /dev/nvme1n1 /data
sudo chown $USER:$USER /data

# Add to /etc/fstab for auto-mount on reboot:
echo '/dev/nvme1n1 /data ext4 defaults,nofail 0 2' | sudo tee -a /etc/fstab

Size: ~25 GB for 7,300 parsed Parquet files. A 50 GB gp3 volume costs ~$4/month and leaves room for growth.

Pre-cache all Parquet files

# Run once to download all parsed files from S3 to the EBS volume
from impulse import ReplayDataset
from impulse.collection.s3_manager import S3Manager

dataset = ReplayDataset(
    db_path='./impulse.db',
    s3_manager=S3Manager(),
    cache_dir='/data/parsed',
)

# Iterate to trigger caching
for i, replay in enumerate(dataset):
    if (i + 1) % 100 == 0:
        print(f"Cached {i + 1}/{len(dataset)}")

After this, all subsequent training runs read from local /data/parsed with no S3 traffic.

Training script structure

# scripts/train.py
import argparse
import torch
import torch.nn as nn
from torch.utils.data import DataLoader

from impulse import (
    ReplayDataset, PreprocessingPipeline,
    FeatureSelector, PhysicalNormalizer, split_replay_ids,
)
from impulse.training import ReplayTrainingDataset


def main(args):
    dataset = ReplayDataset(db_path=args.db_path, cache_dir=args.cache_dir)
    train_ids, val_ids, _ = split_replay_ids(dataset.replay_ids)

    pipeline = PreprocessingPipeline([
        FeatureSelector.from_preset('physics'),
        PhysicalNormalizer(),
    ])
    n_features = len(pipeline.feature_columns)

    train_data = ReplayTrainingDataset(
        dataset, pipeline, train_ids,
        window_size=args.window_size, stride=args.stride,
    )
    val_data = ReplayTrainingDataset(
        dataset, pipeline, val_ids,
        window_size=args.window_size, stride=args.stride,
    )

    train_loader = DataLoader(
        train_data, batch_size=args.batch_size,
        shuffle=True, num_workers=4, pin_memory=True,
    )
    val_loader = DataLoader(
        val_data, batch_size=args.batch_size,
        num_workers=4, pin_memory=True,
    )

    device = torch.device('cuda' if torch.cuda.is_available() else 'cpu')
    model = build_model(args.model, n_features, args).to(device)
    optimizer = torch.optim.Adam(model.parameters(), lr=args.lr)
    loss_fn = nn.MSELoss()

    for epoch in range(args.epochs):
        model.train()
        train_loss = 0
        for batch in train_loader:
            batch = batch.to(device)
            x, y = batch[:, :-1, :], batch[:, -1, :]  # input window, target frame
            pred = model(x)
            loss = loss_fn(pred, y)
            optimizer.zero_grad()
            loss.backward()
            optimizer.step()
            train_loss += loss.item()

        # Validation
        model.eval()
        val_loss = 0
        with torch.no_grad():
            for batch in val_loader:
                batch = batch.to(device)
                x, y = batch[:, :-1, :], batch[:, -1, :]
                pred = model(x)
                val_loss += loss_fn(pred, y).item()

        print(f"Epoch {epoch}: train={train_loss/len(train_loader):.6f} "
              f"val={val_loss/len(val_loader):.6f}")

    torch.save(model.state_dict(), args.output)


def build_model(name, n_features, args):
    if name == 'linear':
        return nn.Linear(n_features * (args.window_size - 1), n_features)
    elif name == 'mlp':
        hidden = args.hidden_size
        input_dim = n_features * (args.window_size - 1)
        return nn.Sequential(
            nn.Linear(input_dim, hidden), nn.ReLU(),
            nn.Linear(hidden, hidden), nn.ReLU(),
            nn.Linear(hidden, n_features),
        )
    elif name == 'lstm':
        class LSTMPredictor(nn.Module):
            def __init__(self):
                super().__init__()
                self.lstm = nn.LSTM(n_features, args.hidden_size, num_layers=2, batch_first=True)
                self.head = nn.Linear(args.hidden_size, n_features)
            def forward(self, x):
                out, _ = self.lstm(x)
                return self.head(out[:, -1, :])
        return LSTMPredictor()
    else:
        raise ValueError(f"Unknown model: {name}")


if __name__ == '__main__':
    parser = argparse.ArgumentParser()
    parser.add_argument('--db-path', default='./impulse.db')
    parser.add_argument('--cache-dir', default='/data/parsed')
    parser.add_argument('--model', default='linear', choices=['linear', 'mlp', 'lstm'])
    parser.add_argument('--window-size', type=int, default=2)
    parser.add_argument('--stride', type=int, default=1)
    parser.add_argument('--batch-size', type=int, default=2048)
    parser.add_argument('--hidden-size', type=int, default=256)
    parser.add_argument('--lr', type=float, default=1e-3)
    parser.add_argument('--epochs', type=int, default=10)
    parser.add_argument('--output', default='model.pt')
    args = parser.parse_args()
    main(args)

Running on EC2

# SSH to EC2 instance
ssh -i key.pem ec2-user@<instance-ip>

# Clone repo and install
git clone <repo-url> impulse && cd impulse
uv sync --extra training

# Sync database
python -c "from impulse.collection.database import ImpulseDB; from impulse.collection.s3_manager import S3Manager; ImpulseDB(s3_manager=S3Manager()).pull()"

# Backfill segment boundaries (first time only)
python scripts/backfill_segment_boundaries.py --db-path ./impulse.db --s3

# Pre-cache Parquet files to EBS (first time only)
python -c "
from impulse import ReplayDataset
from impulse.collection.s3_manager import S3Manager
ds = ReplayDataset(db_path='./impulse.db', s3_manager=S3Manager(), cache_dir='/data/parsed')
for i, r in enumerate(ds):
    if (i+1) % 100 == 0: print(f'{i+1}/{len(ds)}')
"

# Train linear baseline
python scripts/train.py --model linear --window-size 2 --epochs 10

# Train MLP with longer context window
python scripts/train.py --model mlp --window-size 16 --stride 8 --epochs 20

# Train LSTM
python scripts/train.py --model lstm --window-size 64 --stride 16 --batch-size 512 --epochs 30

Stage 4: Sequence Models (LSTM, Transformer)

Key differences from linear/MLP

  • Longer context windows: window_size=64 or 128 instead of 2.
  • Smaller batch sizes: Sequence models use more memory per sample.
  • Gradient clipping: LSTMs can have exploding gradients. Add torch.nn.utils.clip_grad_norm_(model.parameters(), max_norm=1.0).
  • Learning rate scheduling: ReduceLROnPlateau or cosine annealing.

Variable-length segments (LSTM with packing)

For tasks where you want to process entire gameplay segments rather than fixed windows:

from torch.nn.utils.rnn import pad_sequence, pack_padded_sequence

train_data = ReplayTrainingDataset(
    dataset, pipeline, train_ids,
    window_size=None,  # whole segments
)

def pad_collate(batch):
    """Custom collate for variable-length sequences."""
    lengths = torch.tensor([len(seq) for seq in batch])
    padded = pad_sequence(batch, batch_first=True)
    return padded, lengths

loader = DataLoader(train_data, batch_size=32, collate_fn=pad_collate)

for padded_batch, lengths in loader:
    packed = pack_padded_sequence(padded_batch, lengths, batch_first=True, enforce_sorted=False)
    output, (h_n, c_n) = model.lstm(packed)
    # ...

Evaluation

Per-feature MSE comparison

Always compare against the trivial “predict no change” baseline:

# After training, evaluate on test set
pipeline_inv = pipeline  # for denormalization

test_data = ReplayTrainingDataset(dataset, pipeline, test_ids, window_size=2, stride=1)
test_loader = DataLoader(test_data, batch_size=2048)

all_preds, all_targets, all_inputs = [], [], []
model.eval()
with torch.no_grad():
    for batch in test_loader:
        x, y = batch[:, 0, :], batch[:, 1, :]
        pred = model(x.unsqueeze(1) if isinstance(model, LSTMPredictor) else x)
        all_preds.append(pred.cpu())
        all_targets.append(y.cpu())
        all_inputs.append(x.cpu())

preds = torch.cat(all_preds).numpy()
targets = torch.cat(all_targets).numpy()
inputs = torch.cat(all_inputs).numpy()

model_mse = ((targets - preds) ** 2).mean(axis=0)
trivial_mse = ((targets - inputs) ** 2).mean(axis=0)

# Convert back to physical units for interpretable errors
import pandas as pd
pred_df = pd.DataFrame(preds, columns=pipeline.feature_columns)
raw_pred = pipeline.inverse(pred_df)

Experiment tracking

See WANDB.md for full setup instructions and concepts. The short version: every run of scripts/train.py should be wrapped with wandb.init / wandb.finish, with per-epoch losses and final per-feature MSE logged.

import wandb

# At the start of main(), before training:
run = wandb.init(
    project="impulse",
    name=f"{args.model}-full-hidden{args.hidden_size}-window{args.window_size}",
    config=vars(args),
)

# Inside the epoch loop:
wandb.log({
    "epoch": epoch,
    "train_loss": train_loss / len(train_loader),
    "val_loss": val_loss / len(val_loader),
})

# After evaluation on the test set, log per-feature results:
table = wandb.Table(columns=["feature", "trivial_mse", "model_mse", "improvement_pct"])
for i, name in enumerate(pipeline.feature_columns):
    improvement = (1 - model_mse[i] / trivial_mse[i]) * 100
    table.add_data(name, float(trivial_mse[i]), float(model_mse[i]), float(improvement))
wandb.log({"per_feature_mse": table})

# At the end of main():
wandb.finish()

Results are then loaded into presentation notebooks via the W&B API — see WANDB.md for the loading pattern. This means presentation notebooks never re-run training; they only load and visualise saved results.

Cost Estimates

Resource Cost Notes
EBS gp3 50GB ~$4/month Persistent parsed data cache
g4dn.xlarge spot ~$0.16/hr T4 GPU, good for linear/MLP/LSTM
g5.xlarge spot ~$0.40/hr A10G GPU, needed for Transformers
S3 storage (25GB parsed) ~$0.60/month Already stored
Data transfer (first cache) ~$2.25 one-time 25GB at $0.09/GB
Source Code
# Training Workflow

End-to-end guide for training models on Rocket League replay data, from a
local linear baseline on a small sample to full-scale sequence model training
on AWS.

---

## Prerequisites

```bash
# Install base dependencies
uv sync

# Install with PyTorch (required for training module)
uv sync --extra training

# Sync database from S3 (get latest parse state)
python -c "
from impulse.collection.database import ImpulseDB
from impulse.collection.s3_manager import S3Manager
db = ImpulseDB(s3_manager=S3Manager())
db.pull()
"

# Backfill segment boundaries (one-time, after first setup or new parses)
python scripts/backfill_segment_boundaries.py --db-path ./impulse.db --s3
```

---

## Stage 1: Local Linear Baseline (Small Sample)

Train a linear model on ~200 replays locally. No GPU needed. Takes a few
minutes to run.

```python
import numpy as np
from sklearn.linear_model import Ridge

from impulse import (
    ReplayDataset, PreprocessingPipeline,
    FeatureSelector, PhysicalNormalizer, split_replay_ids,
)
from impulse.preprocessing import deserialize_boundaries

# 1. Load dataset and sample replay IDs
dataset = ReplayDataset(db_path='./impulse.db', cache_dir='./replay-cache/')
sample_ids = dataset.sample_replay_ids(200, seed=51)
train_ids, val_ids, test_ids = split_replay_ids(sample_ids)

# 2. Build preprocessing pipeline
pipeline = PreprocessingPipeline([
    FeatureSelector.from_preset('physics'),
    PhysicalNormalizer(),
])

# 3. Build X/Y arrays from training segments
X_train, Y_train = [], []
for replay in dataset.iter_ids(train_ids):
    boundaries = deserialize_boundaries(dataset.replay_info[replay.replay_id]['segment_boundaries'])
    processed = pipeline(replay.frames).to_numpy(np.float32)
    for start, end in boundaries:
        segment = processed[start:end]
        X_train.append(segment[:-1])  # all frames except last
        Y_train.append(segment[1:])   # all frames except first

X_train = np.concatenate(X_train)
Y_train = np.concatenate(Y_train)

# 4. Same for validation
X_val, Y_val = [], []
for replay in dataset.iter_ids(val_ids):
    boundaries = deserialize_boundaries(dataset.replay_info[replay.replay_id]['segment_boundaries'])
    processed = pipeline(replay.frames).to_numpy(np.float32)
    for start, end in boundaries:
        segment = processed[start:end]
        X_val.append(segment[:-1])
        Y_val.append(segment[1:])

X_val = np.concatenate(X_val)
Y_val = np.concatenate(Y_val)

# 5. Trivial baseline: predict no change
trivial_mse = ((Y_val - X_val) ** 2).mean(axis=0)

# 6. Train linear model
model = Ridge(alpha=1.0)
model.fit(X_train, Y_train)

Y_pred = model.predict(X_val)
linear_mse = ((Y_val - Y_pred) ** 2).mean(axis=0)

# 7. Compare
feature_names = pipeline.feature_columns
for i, name in enumerate(feature_names):
    improvement = (1 - linear_mse[i] / trivial_mse[i]) * 100
    print(f"{name:40s}  trivial={trivial_mse[i]:.6f}  linear={linear_mse[i]:.6f}  improvement={improvement:.1f}%")
```

### What to look for

- The linear model should beat the trivial baseline on most features.
- Position features should be easy to predict (smooth trajectories).
- Angular velocity and action flags are harder (discontinuous).
- If the linear model is worse than trivial on some features, that's expected —
  those features may need nonlinear models or longer context windows.

---

## Stage 2: Full Dataset Linear Model (7,300 Replays)

Same approach as Stage 1 but iterating over all replays. Still fits in memory
for a linear model since sklearn Ridge doesn't need all data simultaneously —
but building the full X/Y arrays will be large (~35M frames × 97 features ×
4 bytes ≈ 13 GB).

Options for fitting at scale:

### Option A: Incremental fitting with SGDRegressor

```python
from sklearn.linear_model import SGDRegressor

models = [SGDRegressor(loss='squared_error', alpha=1e-4) for _ in range(n_features)]

for replay in dataset.iter_ids(train_ids):
    boundaries = deserialize_boundaries(dataset.replay_info[replay.replay_id]['segment_boundaries'])
    processed = pipeline(replay.frames).to_numpy(np.float32)
    for start, end in boundaries:
        seg = processed[start:end]
        X, Y = seg[:-1], seg[1:]
        for j, m in enumerate(models):
            m.partial_fit(X, Y[:, j])
```

### Option B: PyTorch linear model (recommended for consistency with later stages)

```python
import torch
import torch.nn as nn
from torch.utils.data import DataLoader
from impulse.training import ReplayTrainingDataset

train_dataset = ReplayTrainingDataset(
    dataset=dataset, pipeline=pipeline,
    replay_ids=train_ids,
    window_size=2, stride=1,  # pairs of consecutive frames
)
loader = DataLoader(train_dataset, batch_size=2048, shuffle=True, num_workers=4)

model = nn.Linear(n_features, n_features)
optimizer = torch.optim.Adam(model.parameters(), lr=1e-3)
loss_fn = nn.MSELoss()

for epoch in range(10):
    total_loss = 0
    for batch in loader:
        x, y = batch[:, 0, :], batch[:, 1, :]  # window_size=2 → input, target
        pred = model(x)
        loss = loss_fn(pred, y)
        optimizer.zero_grad()
        loss.backward()
        optimizer.step()
        total_loss += loss.item()
    print(f"Epoch {epoch}: loss={total_loss / len(loader):.6f}")
```

---

## Stage 3: AWS Setup for GPU Training

### EC2 Instance

Recommended: `g4dn.xlarge` (1x T4 GPU, 4 vCPU, 16 GB RAM, ~$0.53/hr on-demand
or ~$0.16/hr spot).

For larger models: `g5.xlarge` (1x A10G GPU, 24 GB VRAM) or `p3.2xlarge`
(1x V100, 16 GB VRAM).

### Storage: EBS Volume for Parsed Data

Create a persistent EBS gp3 volume to store cached Parquet files. This
survives instance stop/start and avoids re-downloading from S3 each time.

```bash
# On the EC2 instance, after attaching the EBS volume:
sudo mkfs.ext4 /dev/nvme1n1           # format (first time only!)
sudo mkdir -p /data
sudo mount /dev/nvme1n1 /data
sudo chown $USER:$USER /data

# Add to /etc/fstab for auto-mount on reboot:
echo '/dev/nvme1n1 /data ext4 defaults,nofail 0 2' | sudo tee -a /etc/fstab
```

Size: ~25 GB for 7,300 parsed Parquet files. A 50 GB gp3 volume costs ~$4/month
and leaves room for growth.

### Pre-cache all Parquet files

```python
# Run once to download all parsed files from S3 to the EBS volume
from impulse import ReplayDataset
from impulse.collection.s3_manager import S3Manager

dataset = ReplayDataset(
    db_path='./impulse.db',
    s3_manager=S3Manager(),
    cache_dir='/data/parsed',
)

# Iterate to trigger caching
for i, replay in enumerate(dataset):
    if (i + 1) % 100 == 0:
        print(f"Cached {i + 1}/{len(dataset)}")
```

After this, all subsequent training runs read from local `/data/parsed` with
no S3 traffic.

### Training script structure

```python
# scripts/train.py
import argparse
import torch
import torch.nn as nn
from torch.utils.data import DataLoader

from impulse import (
    ReplayDataset, PreprocessingPipeline,
    FeatureSelector, PhysicalNormalizer, split_replay_ids,
)
from impulse.training import ReplayTrainingDataset


def main(args):
    dataset = ReplayDataset(db_path=args.db_path, cache_dir=args.cache_dir)
    train_ids, val_ids, _ = split_replay_ids(dataset.replay_ids)

    pipeline = PreprocessingPipeline([
        FeatureSelector.from_preset('physics'),
        PhysicalNormalizer(),
    ])
    n_features = len(pipeline.feature_columns)

    train_data = ReplayTrainingDataset(
        dataset, pipeline, train_ids,
        window_size=args.window_size, stride=args.stride,
    )
    val_data = ReplayTrainingDataset(
        dataset, pipeline, val_ids,
        window_size=args.window_size, stride=args.stride,
    )

    train_loader = DataLoader(
        train_data, batch_size=args.batch_size,
        shuffle=True, num_workers=4, pin_memory=True,
    )
    val_loader = DataLoader(
        val_data, batch_size=args.batch_size,
        num_workers=4, pin_memory=True,
    )

    device = torch.device('cuda' if torch.cuda.is_available() else 'cpu')
    model = build_model(args.model, n_features, args).to(device)
    optimizer = torch.optim.Adam(model.parameters(), lr=args.lr)
    loss_fn = nn.MSELoss()

    for epoch in range(args.epochs):
        model.train()
        train_loss = 0
        for batch in train_loader:
            batch = batch.to(device)
            x, y = batch[:, :-1, :], batch[:, -1, :]  # input window, target frame
            pred = model(x)
            loss = loss_fn(pred, y)
            optimizer.zero_grad()
            loss.backward()
            optimizer.step()
            train_loss += loss.item()

        # Validation
        model.eval()
        val_loss = 0
        with torch.no_grad():
            for batch in val_loader:
                batch = batch.to(device)
                x, y = batch[:, :-1, :], batch[:, -1, :]
                pred = model(x)
                val_loss += loss_fn(pred, y).item()

        print(f"Epoch {epoch}: train={train_loss/len(train_loader):.6f} "
              f"val={val_loss/len(val_loader):.6f}")

    torch.save(model.state_dict(), args.output)


def build_model(name, n_features, args):
    if name == 'linear':
        return nn.Linear(n_features * (args.window_size - 1), n_features)
    elif name == 'mlp':
        hidden = args.hidden_size
        input_dim = n_features * (args.window_size - 1)
        return nn.Sequential(
            nn.Linear(input_dim, hidden), nn.ReLU(),
            nn.Linear(hidden, hidden), nn.ReLU(),
            nn.Linear(hidden, n_features),
        )
    elif name == 'lstm':
        class LSTMPredictor(nn.Module):
            def __init__(self):
                super().__init__()
                self.lstm = nn.LSTM(n_features, args.hidden_size, num_layers=2, batch_first=True)
                self.head = nn.Linear(args.hidden_size, n_features)
            def forward(self, x):
                out, _ = self.lstm(x)
                return self.head(out[:, -1, :])
        return LSTMPredictor()
    else:
        raise ValueError(f"Unknown model: {name}")


if __name__ == '__main__':
    parser = argparse.ArgumentParser()
    parser.add_argument('--db-path', default='./impulse.db')
    parser.add_argument('--cache-dir', default='/data/parsed')
    parser.add_argument('--model', default='linear', choices=['linear', 'mlp', 'lstm'])
    parser.add_argument('--window-size', type=int, default=2)
    parser.add_argument('--stride', type=int, default=1)
    parser.add_argument('--batch-size', type=int, default=2048)
    parser.add_argument('--hidden-size', type=int, default=256)
    parser.add_argument('--lr', type=float, default=1e-3)
    parser.add_argument('--epochs', type=int, default=10)
    parser.add_argument('--output', default='model.pt')
    args = parser.parse_args()
    main(args)
```

### Running on EC2

```bash
# SSH to EC2 instance
ssh -i key.pem ec2-user@<instance-ip>

# Clone repo and install
git clone <repo-url> impulse && cd impulse
uv sync --extra training

# Sync database
python -c "from impulse.collection.database import ImpulseDB; from impulse.collection.s3_manager import S3Manager; ImpulseDB(s3_manager=S3Manager()).pull()"

# Backfill segment boundaries (first time only)
python scripts/backfill_segment_boundaries.py --db-path ./impulse.db --s3

# Pre-cache Parquet files to EBS (first time only)
python -c "
from impulse import ReplayDataset
from impulse.collection.s3_manager import S3Manager
ds = ReplayDataset(db_path='./impulse.db', s3_manager=S3Manager(), cache_dir='/data/parsed')
for i, r in enumerate(ds):
    if (i+1) % 100 == 0: print(f'{i+1}/{len(ds)}')
"

# Train linear baseline
python scripts/train.py --model linear --window-size 2 --epochs 10

# Train MLP with longer context window
python scripts/train.py --model mlp --window-size 16 --stride 8 --epochs 20

# Train LSTM
python scripts/train.py --model lstm --window-size 64 --stride 16 --batch-size 512 --epochs 30
```

---

## Stage 4: Sequence Models (LSTM, Transformer)

### Key differences from linear/MLP

- **Longer context windows**: `window_size=64` or `128` instead of 2.
- **Smaller batch sizes**: Sequence models use more memory per sample.
- **Gradient clipping**: LSTMs can have exploding gradients.
  Add `torch.nn.utils.clip_grad_norm_(model.parameters(), max_norm=1.0)`.
- **Learning rate scheduling**: `ReduceLROnPlateau` or cosine annealing.

### Variable-length segments (LSTM with packing)

For tasks where you want to process entire gameplay segments rather than
fixed windows:

```python
from torch.nn.utils.rnn import pad_sequence, pack_padded_sequence

train_data = ReplayTrainingDataset(
    dataset, pipeline, train_ids,
    window_size=None,  # whole segments
)

def pad_collate(batch):
    """Custom collate for variable-length sequences."""
    lengths = torch.tensor([len(seq) for seq in batch])
    padded = pad_sequence(batch, batch_first=True)
    return padded, lengths

loader = DataLoader(train_data, batch_size=32, collate_fn=pad_collate)

for padded_batch, lengths in loader:
    packed = pack_padded_sequence(padded_batch, lengths, batch_first=True, enforce_sorted=False)
    output, (h_n, c_n) = model.lstm(packed)
    # ...
```

---

## Evaluation

### Per-feature MSE comparison

Always compare against the trivial "predict no change" baseline:

```python
# After training, evaluate on test set
pipeline_inv = pipeline  # for denormalization

test_data = ReplayTrainingDataset(dataset, pipeline, test_ids, window_size=2, stride=1)
test_loader = DataLoader(test_data, batch_size=2048)

all_preds, all_targets, all_inputs = [], [], []
model.eval()
with torch.no_grad():
    for batch in test_loader:
        x, y = batch[:, 0, :], batch[:, 1, :]
        pred = model(x.unsqueeze(1) if isinstance(model, LSTMPredictor) else x)
        all_preds.append(pred.cpu())
        all_targets.append(y.cpu())
        all_inputs.append(x.cpu())

preds = torch.cat(all_preds).numpy()
targets = torch.cat(all_targets).numpy()
inputs = torch.cat(all_inputs).numpy()

model_mse = ((targets - preds) ** 2).mean(axis=0)
trivial_mse = ((targets - inputs) ** 2).mean(axis=0)

# Convert back to physical units for interpretable errors
import pandas as pd
pred_df = pd.DataFrame(preds, columns=pipeline.feature_columns)
raw_pred = pipeline.inverse(pred_df)
```

### Experiment tracking

See `WANDB.md` for full setup instructions and concepts. The short version:
every run of `scripts/train.py` should be wrapped with `wandb.init` /
`wandb.finish`, with per-epoch losses and final per-feature MSE logged.

```python
import wandb

# At the start of main(), before training:
run = wandb.init(
    project="impulse",
    name=f"{args.model}-full-hidden{args.hidden_size}-window{args.window_size}",
    config=vars(args),
)

# Inside the epoch loop:
wandb.log({
    "epoch": epoch,
    "train_loss": train_loss / len(train_loader),
    "val_loss": val_loss / len(val_loader),
})

# After evaluation on the test set, log per-feature results:
table = wandb.Table(columns=["feature", "trivial_mse", "model_mse", "improvement_pct"])
for i, name in enumerate(pipeline.feature_columns):
    improvement = (1 - model_mse[i] / trivial_mse[i]) * 100
    table.add_data(name, float(trivial_mse[i]), float(model_mse[i]), float(improvement))
wandb.log({"per_feature_mse": table})

# At the end of main():
wandb.finish()
```

Results are then loaded into presentation notebooks via the W&B API — see
`WANDB.md` for the loading pattern. This means presentation notebooks never
re-run training; they only load and visualise saved results.

---

## Cost Estimates

| Resource | Cost | Notes |
|----------|------|-------|
| EBS gp3 50GB | ~$4/month | Persistent parsed data cache |
| g4dn.xlarge spot | ~$0.16/hr | T4 GPU, good for linear/MLP/LSTM |
| g5.xlarge spot | ~$0.40/hr | A10G GPU, needed for Transformers |
| S3 storage (25GB parsed) | ~$0.60/month | Already stored |
| Data transfer (first cache) | ~$2.25 one-time | 25GB at $0.09/GB |
